---
title: "12.2 Rust arrays, ownership, and mutation"
---

## Explanation

After reading [Learn Rust](https://rust-lang.org/learn/), inspect how Rust
represents one-dimensional numerical data. A `Vec<f64>` owns a growable
contiguous array of floating-point values. A slice such as `&[f64]` or
`&mut [f64]` lets a function use part of an array without taking ownership of
the whole vector.

Rust asks you to say, in the function signature, whether a function only reads
data, mutates data, or creates new data:

```rust
fn sum(xs: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    xs.iter().sum()
}

fn scale_inplace(xs: &mut [f64], a: f64) {
    for x in xs.iter_mut() {
        *x *= a;
    }
}

fn shifted(xs: &[f64], b: f64) -> Vec<f64> {
    xs.iter().map(|x| x + b).collect()
}
```

Here `&[f64]` means a read-only slice, `&mut [f64]` means a mutable slice, and
`Vec<f64>` means the function returns a newly owned vector. The function
signature tells the reader and the compiler whether the function can modify the
input or allocates a new result.

Do not say that Rust forbids all aliasing. Rust allows many immutable aliases:
several parts of the program can read the same data. What safe Rust prevents is
uncontrolled mutable aliasing. In safe Rust, you can have many immutable
references or one mutable reference, but not arbitrary mutation through several
aliases at the same time.

For first examples, use `Vec<f64>` and slices. For multidimensional arrays,
Rust projects often use a crate such as `ndarray`, but the same basic question
remains: which function owns the data, which function only reads it, which
function mutates it, and which function allocates a new result?

## Things to look up

- Rust slice
- `Vec`
- Borrowing
- Mutable reference
- Aliasing

## Exercise

Ask an AI agent to create a tiny Rust module with these functions:

- `sum(xs: &[f64]) -> f64`
- `scale_inplace(xs: &mut [f64], a: f64)`
- `shifted(xs: &[f64], b: f64) -> Vec<f64>`

Add unit tests for all three functions.

In your answer, explain:

- which functions can mutate the input,
- where that fact appears in the code,
- what `&[f64]` means,
- what `&mut [f64]` means,
- which function allocates a new vector,
- why safe Rust prevents uncontrolled mutable aliasing.

Then ask the agent for one short example that the Rust compiler rejects because
the code tries to borrow the same vector mutably in two places at once. Explain
what rule the compiler is enforcing.

## Notes for the exercise

- Do not write that all aliasing is impossible in Rust.
- Focus on function signatures and mutation, not on advanced Rust syntax.
- Treat `ndarray` as a later tool for multidimensional arrays, not as the first
  concept to learn.
